steps.title: Set up the x-axis crossing equation
steps.content:
Finding $x$-axis crossings means solving $y = 0$:
$$0 = 9\left(\frac{a}{6}\right)^{x+c} - b$$
$$9\left(\frac{a}{6}\right)^{x+c} = b$$
We need to determine how many real solutions exist.

steps.title: Identify the base and monotonicity
steps.content: Since $a > 6$, we have $\frac{a}{6} > 1$. The function $f(x) = 9\left(\frac{a}{6}\right)^{x+c}$ is an exponential with base greater than 1, so it is strictly increasing and continuous for all real $x$.

steps.title: Apply the monotonicity principle
steps.content:
A strictly increasing, continuous function equals any value in its range exactly once.

The exponential $f(x) = 9\left(\frac{a}{6}\right)^{x+c}$ has range $(0, \infty)$.

Since $b > 0$, the value $b$ lies within this range. Therefore, the equation $f(x) = b$ has exactly one solution.

That matches choice B — B is correct.
